name: PaperLibrary Public Domain Starter Pack
rights_note: Project Gutenberg records these works as public domain in the USA. Public-domain status differs by jurisdiction; release packaging should keep this note visible.

items.title: Pride and Prejudice
items.description: Jane Austen's 1813 comedy of manners: Elizabeth Bennet and the proud Mr Darcy spar their way from prejudice to love in Regency England.

items.title: Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus
items.description: Mary Shelley's 1818 gothic novel: a young scientist animates a creature from dead matter, then abandons it — with tragic consequences.

items.title: Moby-Dick; or, The Whale
items.description: Herman Melville's 1851 epic of Captain Ahab's monomaniacal hunt for the white whale — a vast meditation on obsession, nature, and fate.

items.title: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland
items.description: Lewis Carroll's 1865 nonsense classic: Alice tumbles down a rabbit-hole into a logic-bending world of talking creatures and mad tea-parties.

items.title: The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes
items.description: Twelve of Arthur Conan Doyle's 1892 stories introducing the great detective and his method of ruthless deduction, narrated by Dr Watson.

items.title: The Time Machine
items.description: H. G. Wells's 1895 novella: a Victorian inventor travels to the year 802,701 and finds humanity split into the gentle Eloi and subterranean Morlocks.

items.title: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave
items.description: The 1845 autobiography of Frederick Douglass, who escaped slavery to become a towering abolitionist orator and writer.

items.title: The Souls of Black Folk
items.description: W. E. B. Du Bois's 1903 essays on race in America, introducing 'double consciousness' and the metaphor of 'the veil'.

items.title: On the Origin of Species By Means of Natural Selection
items.description: Charles Darwin's 1859 work setting out evolution by natural selection — among the most influential books in the history of science.

items.title: Walden, and On The Duty Of Civil Disobedience
items.description: Henry David Thoreau's 1854 account of two years living simply by Walden Pond — a founding text of self-reliance and nature writing.

items.title: Leaves of Grass
items.description: Walt Whitman's ever-expanding book of poems (from 1855), a sprawling democratic hymn to the body, the self, and America.

items.title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz
items.description: L. Frank Baum's 1900 fairy tale: a Kansas girl and her dog are swept to the land of Oz and journey to the Emerald City.
